data.html:
Key Points

This level has provided many details about insurance products and practices. Since we've addressed so much information, let's review some of this level's "key points." If you struggle to understand any of these key points, you should study the level again.

  • HIPAA's Privacy Rule explains when people's health information can or can't be shared.

  • HIPAA's Security Rule explains how people's medical information must be safeguarded.

  • HIPAA's Privacy Rule applies to "covered entities."

  • Covered entities under HIPAA are health care providers, health plans, and health care clearinghouses.

  • Business associates of covered entities (such as health insurance producers) must comply with HIPAA's rules.

  • Health information is only protected by HIPAA if it could reasonably be used to identify someone.

  • Information that can't reasonably be linked to a specific person might not be protected by HIPAA.

  • Although HIPAA might technically not apply to life insurance, life insurance companies often must follow similar or stricter rules set by state law.

  • Before being allowed to access protected health information on behalf of covered entities, business associates must sign a business associate agreement.

  • Covered entities might not need permission to share medical information in a medical emergency or to receive payment for care.

  • HIPAA allows you to obtain copies of your medical records at a nominal cost.

  • You typically can't be denied access to your own health information.

  • Covered entities need to track the disclosure of people's health records.

  • If you are incapacitated, your health information can be disclosed to your personal representative on a need-to-know basis.

  • Your personal representative is someone who will either be making medical decisions for you or will be responsible for your care.

  • Different HIPAA rules apply to fully insured group health plans vs. self-insured group health plans.

  • HIPAA and the HITECH Act limit how your medical information can be used for marketing purposes.

  • Despite HIPAA rules, covered entities might be able to share health information with the courts and government authorities.

  • HIPAA's security rules are intentionally general and don't require the use of specific technology.

  • After a security breach involving protected health information, covered entities might be required to report the incident to impacted parties.
data.markdown:
# Key Points
This level has provided many details about insurance products and practices. Since we've addressed so much information, let's review some of this level's "key points." If you struggle to understand any of these key points, you should study the level again.

- HIPAA's Privacy Rule explains when people's health information can or can't be shared.
- HIPAA's Security Rule explains how people's medical information must be safeguarded.
- HIPAA's Privacy Rule applies to "covered entities."
- Covered entities under HIPAA are health care providers, health plans, and health care clearinghouses.
- Business associates of covered entities (such as health insurance producers) must comply with HIPAA's rules.
- Health information is only protected by HIPAA if it could reasonably be used to identify someone.
- Information that can't reasonably be linked to a specific person might not be protected by HIPAA.
- Although HIPAA might technically not apply to life insurance, life insurance companies often must follow similar or stricter rules set by state law.
- Before being allowed to access protected health information on behalf of covered entities, business associates must sign a business associate agreement.
- Covered entities might not need permission to share medical information in a medical emergency or to receive payment for care.
- HIPAA allows you to obtain copies of your medical records at a nominal cost.
- You typically can't be denied access to your own health information.
- Covered entities need to track the disclosure of people's health records.
- If you are incapacitated, your health information can be disclosed to your personal representative on a need-to-know basis.
- Your personal representative is someone who will either be making medical decisions for you or will be responsible for your care.
- Different HIPAA rules apply to fully insured group health plans vs. self-insured group health plans.
- HIPAA and the HITECH Act limit how your medical information can be used for marketing purposes.
- Despite HIPAA rules, covered entities might be able to share health information with the courts and government authorities.
- HIPAA's security rules are intentionally general and don't require the use of specific technology.
- After a security breach involving protected health information, covered entities might be required to report the incident to impacted parties.
